author_cultural_context: Vajrayana Buddhism; Mahayana ritual and iconography
author_bio: Abhayākaragupta was a 12th-century Indian Buddhist master and scholar-monk, renowned for works on tantric ritual and iconography, such as the Niṣpaṇṇayogāvali, a key text for mandala construction.
author_notes: Attested in Tibetan Buddhist sources; name and works well established.

author_cultural_context: Jain; Sanskrit courtly poetry and retellings
author_bio: Abhinanda was a Sanskrit poet and scholar, known for the Kādambarīkathāsāra (a poetic abridgement of Bāṇa's Kādambarī) and the Rāmacaritamahākāvya. He belongs to the tradition of Jain-influenced kāvya.
author_notes: May be conflated with another Abhinanda; dates approximate based on style and thematic content.

author_bio: Abhinavagupta was a towering figure in Indian philosophy and aesthetics, active in 10th–11th-century Kashmir. His vast corpus includes the Tantrāloka and Paramārthasāra, and his commentaries shaped the Pratyabhijñā school and Sanskrit literary theory.
author_notes: Well-documented historical polymath; central to Kashmir Shaivism and Indian aesthetics.
representative_titles: Paramārthasāra | Tantrāloka | Parātriṃśikāvivaraṇa | Mālinīślokavārttika | Kramastotra | Paryantapañcāśikā | Ghaṭakarparavivṛti | Anuttarāṣṭikā

author_bio: The Advayaśatikā ('One Hundred and Fifty Verses on Non-duality') is a short Prajñāpāramitā sūtra, part of the vast corpus of Mahāyāna Buddhist scriptures. It emphasizes the non-dual nature of reality and the perfection of wisdom.
author_notes: The author_display_name is the title of a text; no human author is attributed. Categorized as work_title per instructions.

author_bio: Agnisvāmin is a Sanskrit commentator known for his *Lāṭyāyanaśrautasūtrabhāṣya*, a commentary on the Lāṭyāyana Śrautasūtra pertaining to the Sāmaveda. His work is essential for understanding the ritual procedures of the Sāmavedic tradition.
author_notes: Little is known of Agnisvāmin's life. The commentary is attributed and is an important source for the ritual tradition, but the author's historicity is inferred primarily from textual references.

author_cultural_context: Founder of the Nyāya school of philosophy
author_bio: Akṣapāda Gautama (also known as Gautama or Gotama) is the legendary founder of the Nyāya school of logic, credited with composing the Nyāyasūtra. Though details of his life are obscure, his work has been foundational for Indian logic and epistemology.
author_notes: Attested in philosophical tradition; the Nyāyasūtra is securely assigned to this figure, though later commentaries sometimes differ on details of his life.

author_bio: Amarasiṃha was an early Indian lexicographer, traditionally dated to the Gupta period, best known for composing the Amarakośa, a thesaurus and lexicon that became a standard reference for Sanskrit poets and scholars.
author_notes: Well-established figure in Indian lexicography; author of a foundational thesaurus.

author_bio: Amaru is a celebrated Sanskrit poet, traditionally credited with the Amaruśataka, a collection of a hundred erotic verses. Little is known of his life, and he has become a semi-legendary figure in literary tradition, often identified with the king of the same name.
author_notes: Attribution of the Amaruśataka to a single author named Amaru is widespread but unverifiable; the poet's historicity is uncertain.

author_bio: Annambhaṭṭa was a 17th-century Indian philosopher and logician, best known for his foundational textbook Tarkasaṃgraha ('Compendium of Logic'), which systematically presents the principles of Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika thought and remains a standard introductory text in Sanskrit philosophical education.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure in Indian philosophical traditions; his work is widely studied.

author_bio: A default heading for unidentified authors. The works attributed here lack a known author.
author_notes: Used for works where authorship is unknown or unrecoverable. May include multiple hands.

author_bio: Appayadīkṣita was a prolific scholar and poet in Sanskrit, known for his works on Advaita Vedanta, poetics, and devotional poetry. His Vairāgyaśatakam is a collection of verses on renunciation, reflecting his philosophical depth.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure with numerous extant works. The Vairāgyaśatakam is often compared with the Bhartṛhari collection.

author_bio: Founder of the Yogācāra school of Buddhist philosophy, brother of Vasubandhu; prolific author of foundational treatises including the Mahāyānasūtrālaṃkāra and Abhidharmasamuccaya.
author_notes: Well-attested in Buddhist canonical sources; traditional biography includes visionary elements but core historicity is secure.
representative_titles: Śrāvakabhūmi | Mahāyānasūtrālaṃkāra | Abhidharmasamuccaya | Śarīrārthagāthā | Triśatikāyāḥ prajñāpāramitāyāḥ kārikāsaptatiḥ

author_bio: The Aṣṭasāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā ('Perfection of Wisdom in Eight Thousand Lines') is a foundational Mahāyāna sūtra, traditionally considered a revealed text rather than the work of a named human author. It introduced key concepts such as śūnyatā and the bodhisattva path.
author_notes: The text stands as its own attributed 'author' in scholarly catalogs due to the absence of a named human author; it is a canonical sūtra.

author_bio: Aśvaghoṣa was a Buddhist philosopher, poet, and playwright, regarded as one of the greatest Sanskrit literary figures. He authored the Buddhacarita (life of the Buddha), Saundarānanda (a poem on Nanda's conversion), and other works that blend Buddhist doctrine with high literary artistry.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure, often considered the founder of Sanskrit kāvya literature in a Buddhist context.

author_cultural_context: Tantric / Nātha yogic tradition, possibly Bengal or Eastern India
author_bio: Avadhūtasiddha is a name associated with a tantric yogin and author of the *Bhagavadbhaktistotra*, a devotional hymn blending Śaiva and Vaiṣṇava elements. The name 'Avadhūtasiddha' suggests an accomplished (siddha) ascetic (avadhūta), and the figure may be semi-legendary, with hagiographical traditions rather than clear historical records.
author_notes: Limited attestation; 'Avadhūtasiddha' is likely a title or epithet rather than a personal name. Historical details are sparse.

author_bio: Ādiśeṣa is the cosmic serpent in Hindu mythology, often depicted as the couch of Viṣṇu. The Paramārthasāra, attributed to him, is a philosophical text, but the attribution is purely mythological and symbolic.
author_notes: The attribution of the Paramārthasāra to Ādiśeṣa is a literary convention, not a historical claim. The work is likely a late composition placed in the mouth of the divine serpent.

author_bio: Ādyanātha is a legendary figure in the Nath tradition, often regarded as an early teacher or siddha. The authorship of the Anuttaraprakāśapañcāśikā is attributed to him, though historical details are inseparable from hagiography.
author_notes: Attribution to Ādyanātha is traditional; the work belongs to the Nath corpus and may be a compilation from different hands.

author_bio: Sanskrit poet and historian, author of the Vallālacarita, a biographical poem about the Sena king Vallālasena.
author_notes: Known mainly from his single extant work; details of life uncertain.

author_bio: Āryadeva was a Buddhist philosopher of the Madhyamaka school and a disciple of Nāgārjuna. His works include the Caryāmelāvaṇapradīpa, a manual on monastic conduct and yogic practice, as well as the Catuḥśataka, a key Madhyamaka text.
author_notes: Attribution of the Caryāmelāvaṇapradīpa to Āryadeva is debated; it may be a later Tibetan compilation. The two works recorded may represent distinct authors with the same name.

author_bio: Āryaśūra is a celebrated Buddhist poet and monk known for his *Jātakamālā*, a collection of verses and narratives on the Buddha's previous births. His work blends narrative sophistication with devotional fervor and is a landmark in classical Sanskrit kāvya.
author_notes: Attested in Tibetan and Chinese Buddhist sources as a teacher and poet. The *Jātakamālā* was widely translated and influenced later Buddhist literature.

author_bio: Īśvarakṛṣṇa is the author of the *Sāṃkhyakārikā*, the definitive foundational text of the Sāṃkhya school of Indian philosophy. Composed in 72 kārikās, it systematically expounds dualistic realism and influenced later commentaries such as the *Sāṃkhyatattvakaumudī*.
author_notes: Little is known of Īśvarakṛṣṇa beyond his text, but the *Sāṃkhyakārikā* is securely attributed and widely commented upon. He is distinct from the earlier Sāṃkhya teacher Vārsaganya.

author_bio: Uddaṇḍa Śāstrī was a Sanskrit poet and scholar from South India, best known for his poetic message-poem Kokilasaṃdeśa. He flourished in the Vijayanagara period.
author_notes: Attested by internal evidence; limited biographical data.

author_bio: This grouping covers texts transmitted anonymously or without a known author. It includes foundational Vedic collections (Ṛgveda, Maitrāyaṇī Saṃhitā, etc.), Upaniṣads, and Dharmaśāstra works such as Manusmṛti. These works reflect the cumulative efforts of multiple hands across centuries.
author_notes: 'Unknown' is used as an anonymous label for a large corpus of texts where no single author can be identified. Preference is given to this label over 'tradition' because the works are not attributed to any single named tradition in a consistent way.
representative_titles: Ṛgveda | Maitrāyaṇīsaṃhitā | Manusmṛti | Chāndogyopaniṣad | Bṛhadāraṇyakopaniṣad | ChUp-Olivelle | Śatapathabrāhmaṇa | Taittirīyasaṃhitā

author_bio: Utpaladeva was a Kashmiri philosopher and poet, a key figure in the Pratyabhijñā (Recognition) school of non-dual Śaivism. He authored the *Īśvarapratyabhijñākārikā* and the devotional *Śivastotrāvali*, blending rigorous theology with lyrical devotion.
author_notes: Securely historical within the Kashmir Śaiva lineage. His works are central to the Pratyabhijñā corpus.

author_bio: Kalhaṇa was a Kashmiri Brahmin and poet, best known for his historical chronicle Rājataraṅgiṇī, and also credited with the Ardhanarīśvarastotra, a hymn to the androgynous form of Śiva.
author_notes: Well-documented as the author of the Rājataraṅgiṇī; the Ardhanarīśvarastotra attribution is plausible.

author_bio: Kālidāsa is the foremost poet and dramatist of classical Sanskrit, author of epics Raghuvaṃśa and Kumārasaṃbhava, the lyric Meghadūta, and plays such as Abhijñānaśākuntala. His work epitomizes the Gupta period.
author_notes: Firmly historical; exact dates debated but generally assigned to Gupta era.

author_bio: Indian Buddhist philosopher, disciple of Śāntarakṣita, key figure in the early transmission of Buddhism to Tibet; author of Madhyamakāloka and other works on Madhyamaka.
author_notes: Historical figure documented in Tibetan and Sanskrit sources; well-established chronology.

author_cultural_context: Buddhist tantric tradition, possibly associated with the Yogācāra school
author_bio: Kambalapāda is traditionally regarded as a Buddhist tantric master and philosopher, known for authorship of the Navaślokī, a concise text on Buddhist doctrine. His historicity is largely derived from traditional sources and hagiographical accounts.
author_notes: Attribution is traditional; independent historical verification is limited. The name may also appear in Tibetan Buddhist sources.

author_bio: Kapila is the legendary founder of the Sāṃkhya school of Hindu philosophy, traditionally credited with the Sāṃkhyasūtra. His historicity is uncertain, but he is a central figure in the philosophical tradition.
author_notes: Traditional attribution; the Sāṃkhyasūtra is likely a later composition.

author_cultural_context: Maurya Empire, ancient Indian political theory
author_bio: Kauṭilya, also known as Cāṇakya or Viṣṇugupta, is the traditionally credited author of the Arthaśāstra, a foundational treatise on statecraft, economics, and military strategy.
author_notes: Historical reality debated in detail but widely accepted as a key figure in Mauryan administration. Some scholars propose multiple layers of composition.

author_bio: Kavirājayati is a minor Sanskrit author known for the Sāṃkhyatattvapradīpa, a commentary on Sāṃkhya philosophy. Details of his life are obscure.
author_notes: Very little is known; the name suggests a title (Kaviraj) and may not be a personal name.

author_bio: Kedārabhaṭṭa is the author of the Vṛttaratnākara, a celebrated Sanskrit prosody manual that systematically classifies meters. It remains a standard reference for Sanskrit poetry.
author_notes: Often cited by name in metrical treatises. The Vṛttaratnākara is widely used in traditional learning contexts.

author_cultural_context: Hindu ritual (Kauśika Sūtra tradition) and Sāṃkhya philosophy
author_bio: Keśava was a Sanskrit scholar active in Kashmir, known for his commentary on the Kauśikasūtra (a ritual text) and for the Sāṃkhyatattvapradīpikā, a work on Sāṃkhya philosophy. His writings reflect the integration of ritual practice and philosophical inquiry.
author_notes: The name Keśava is relatively common; authorship attributions rely on textual and regional associations without extensive biographical evidence.

author_bio: Kṛṣṇadāsa is known for the Bhāvanāsārasaṃgraha, a work on devotional meditation. The name is common, making precise identification difficult; he may have been a follower of the Gaudiya or Sahajiya traditions.
author_notes: Multiple authors named Kṛṣṇadāsa exist. This attribution is based on the available manuscript of the Bhāvanāsārasaṃgraha.

author_bio: Kṣemarāja was a disciple of Abhinavagupta and a key exponent of Kashmir Śaivism. His works, including Bodhavilāsa and Bhairavānukaraṇastotra, are important for the study of Śaiva philosophy and devotional poetry.
author_notes: Historically attested as a direct disciple of Abhinavagupta.

author_cultural_context: Kashmiri Brahmin, Śaiva and literary polymath
author_bio: Kṣemendra was a prolific Kashmiri Sanskrit author, poet, and literary theorist of the 11th century. He composed epic summaries, narrative cycles, didactic works, and satires, including the *Bhāratamañjarī* and *Avadānakalpalatā*, showcasing his encyclopedic range and engagement with both high and popular literature.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure with a large and varied corpus. Multiple works listed reflect his breadth.
representative_titles: Bhāratamañjarī | Sudhanakinnaryavadāna | Avadānakalpalatā | Kalāvilāsa | Darpadalana | Caturvargasaṃgraha | Sumāgadhāvadāna | Sevyasevakopadeśaḥ

author_bio: Kulaśekhara is one of the twelve Āḻvārs, the poet-saints of the Śrīvaiṣṇava tradition. He is credited with the *Mukundamālā*, a Sanskrit hymn of ten verses in praise of Viṣṇu, and also with Tamil works. His hymns express intense devotion and are recited in temples.
author_notes: Kulaśekhara is historically attested in the Āḻvār tradition. The *Mukundamālā* is widely transmitted in Śrīvaiṣṇava circles.

author_bio: Gāṅgeśa Upādhyāya was a Mithila-based philosopher and the founder of the Navya-Nyāya (New Logic) school. His magnum opus, the *Tattvacintāmaṇi*, revolutionized Indian logic and epistemology, introducing rigorous analytical methods that dominated Sanskrit philosophy for centuries.
author_notes: Gāṅgeśa is securely historical, with a well-documented lineage. The *Tattvacintāmaṇi* generated an extensive commentarial tradition.

author_cultural_context: Advaita Vedānta, early medieval Indian philosophy
author_bio: Gauḍapāda is an early medieval Indian philosopher credited with the Gauḍapādīya Kārikā (verses on the Māṇḍūkya Upaniṣad), a foundational text of Advaita Vedānta. His work systematizes non-dualist teachings and influences later thinkers like Śaṅkara.
author_notes: Often identified as the teacher of Śaṅkara's teacher Govinda. Some traditions blur the historical record; direct textual evidence confirms his authorship.

author_bio: Gorakṣanātha is a legendary yogi and the traditional founder of the Nātha sect. He is credited with the Gorakṣaśataka, a text on haṭha yoga.
author_notes: Widely revered in yogic traditions; historical details are unclear and blended with hagiography.

author_bio: Govardhana is the author of the Āryāsaptaśatī, a collection of śataka (700-verse) poetry in āryā meter, focusing on erotic and courtly themes. His work is part of the bhakti-śṛṅgāra tradition.
author_notes: Dates approximate; place and sect uncertain. Some traditions associate him with Bengal or Mithila.

author_bio: Govind Kaul is a Kashmiri Śaiva scholar, author of the Pradeśasaṃgraha, a compendium of esoteric teachings.
author_notes: Attested in manuscript traditions; limited external biographical evidence.

author_bio: Candragomin was a Buddhist monk and Sanskrit grammarian, author of the Śiṣyalekha, a didactic letter, and a commentary on the Kātantra grammar.
author_notes: Traditionally associated with Nālandā; limited external confirmation.

author_bio: Candrakīrti is an eminent Buddhist philosopher and commentator, a key exponent of the Madhyamaka school. His *Prasannapadā* is a celebrated commentary on Nāgārjuna's *Mūlamadhyamakakārikā*, foundational for understanding Buddhist emptiness philosophy.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; the word count here is for a single extant fragment, not the full work.

author_bio: Caraka is the traditional author of the Carakasaṃhitā, one of the foundational texts of Āyurveda, covering medicine, diagnosis, and therapeutics.
author_notes: Attribution to Caraka as a redactor/author; the text is a composite of earlier schools.

author_cultural_context: Hindu; courtly and devotional poetry; also a grammatician and commentator
author_bio: Jagannātha was a Sanskrit poet and scholar, known for the Sudhālaharī (a commentary on the Amaraughaśāsana) and the Prāṇābharaṇaṭippaṇī. His works blend grammatical analysis with literary commentary.
author_notes: Not to be confused with the better-known Jagannātha Paṇḍitarāja; distinct author with narrower textual footprint.

author_bio: Jaimini is the foundational sage of the Mīmāṃsā school, credited with composing the Mīmāṃsāsūtra, a seminal text on Vedic ritual exegesis and hermeneutics.
author_notes: Distinct from the epic figure; works are philosophical and ritual in nature.

author_bio: Jalhaṇa is a Sanskrit author known for the Mugdhopadeśa, a collection of moral and practical instructions.
author_notes: Little biographical information survives; the Mugdhopadeśa is his primary work.

author_bio: Jayadeva is the celebrated Sanskrit poet of the Gītagovinda, a lyrical poem on the love of Rādhā and Kṛṣṇa. He is also credited with the Candrāloka, a work on poetics.
author_notes: Well-attested historically; his Gītagovinda is a landmark in Sanskrit literature.

author_bio: Jayarakṣita was a Digambara Jain monk and scholar, best known for his commentary Sphuṭārthā Śrīghanācārasaṃgrahaṭīkā on the Śrīghanācārasaṃgraha.
author_notes: Manuscript evidence is limited; the works survive in a few recensions.

author_bio: Indian philosopher and author of the Tattvopaplavasiṃha, a radical sceptical text that critiques knowledge systems, associated with the Cārvāka or materialist school.
author_notes: Known almost solely from his single extant work; identity and details uncertain.

author_bio: Jitāri was a Buddhist scholar known for his works on logic and epistemology. He wrote the Hetutattvopadeśa and other treatises in the pramāṇa tradition.
author_notes: Biographical details are limited; the attribution is based on colophons and internal references.

author_bio: Jīva Gosvāmī was a prominent Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇava theologian and philosopher, authoring numerous works on bhakti and the philosophy of Kṛṣṇa devotion. He played a key role in systematizing the theology of Chaitanya Mahaprabhu.
author_notes: Sometimes referred to as Jīva Gosvāmin. His works include the Ṣaṭsandarbhas, exploring the nature of ultimate reality and devotional practice.

author_bio: Jonarāja was a Kashmiri historian who continued Kalhaṇa's Rājataraṅginī. A separate 'Pseudo-Jonarāja' is a later, possibly spurious, attribution.
author_notes: The composite entry reflects ambiguity between two layers of authorship; the Pseudo-Jonarāja label denotes a later continuation often conflated.

author_bio: Jyotirīśvarakaviśekhara was a Maithili poet and scholar, known for the erotic manual Pañcasāyaka, a treatise on kāmaśāstra in Sanskrit.
author_notes: The name indicates a title ('king of poets'); his identity is tied to the Mithila region.

author_bio: Dāmodaragupta was a Kashmiri poet and minister, known for his satirical and didactic poem Kuṭṭanīmata (The Advice of a Procuress), which offers insights into urban life and social mores.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; the Kuṭṭanīmata is a unique work of kāvya with a strong narrative element.

author_bio: Daṇḍin was an early Indian literary theorist and storyteller, author of the Kāvyādarśa, a foundational work on Sanskrit poetics, and the Daśakumāracarita, a pioneering work of prose fiction.
author_notes: Secure historical attribution; major figure in Sanskrit literary criticism.

author_bio: Dārila is an early medieval commentator on the Kauśikasūtra, a text of the Atharvavedic ritual corpus. His bhāṣya represents one of the earliest systematic expositions of domestic Vedic rites.
author_notes: Attested only through his commentary; biographical details are inferred from textual evidence.

author_cultural_context: Hindu; ritual and domestic works; probable Mithila or Bengal region
author_bio: Devapāla is a medieval commentator known for the Kāṭhagṛhyasūtrabhāṣya, a gloss on the domestic ritual sūtras of the Kāṭha school. His work reflects the scholastic tradition of Vedic ritual exegesis.
author_notes: Some uncertainty exists regarding the precise regional and chronological placement; distinct from the Pāla king of the same name.

author_cultural_context: Indian subcontinent; Buddhist logic and epistemology, Yogācāra school
author_bio: Diṅnāga was a pioneering Buddhist logician who established the tradition of Indian logic (hetuśāstra) with his work Pramāṇasamuccaya. His compact Prajñāpāramitāpiṇḍārtha is a concise summary of the Perfection of Wisdom literature.
author_notes: His major works are fragmentary in Sanskrit but preserved in Tibetan and Chinese; the attribution of the Prajñāpāramitāpiṇḍārtha is secure.

author_bio: Dhanvin is known for a commentary on the Drāhyāyaṇaśrautasūtra, indicating expertise in the sūtra literature of the Sāmaveda.
author_notes: Name is rare and works point to a specialized ritual scholar.

author_cultural_context: Indian subcontinent; Indian Buddhist logic and epistemology
author_bio: Dharmakīrti was the most influential Buddhist logician after Diṅnāga, authoring the monumental Pramāṇavārttika and other works on inference, debate, and epistemology. He reformulated the entire Buddhist logical system.
author_notes: His works are central to the Tibetan Buddhist curriculum; biographical traditions vary, but his historicity is not in doubt.

author_bio: Dhyānacandragosvāmin was a Gaudiya Vaiṣṇava author known for ritual manuals dedicated to the worship of Gaura and Govinda. His work reflects the devotional practices of the Chaitanya tradition.
author_notes: Name suggests a Gosvāmī title; works are niche within the Gauḍīya corpus.

author_bio: Nāgārjuna is the foundational philosopher of the Madhyamaka school of Buddhism. His Mūlamadhyamakārikāḥ present the doctrine of emptiness (śūnyatā) through rigorous dialectical reasoning, influencing Mahāyāna thought across Asia.
author_notes: Highly attested figure; attribution of the Ratnāvalī and Dharmasaṃgraha is sometimes debated but widely accepted in tradition.

author_bio: Nagnajit is traditionally regarded as an ancient authority on painting and iconometry, credited with the Citralakṣaṇam, a treatise on the proportions and symbolism of images.
author_notes: Historicity uncertain; name appears in later art-technical literature as a mythical authority.

author_cultural_context: Hindu devotional and philosophical traditions, likely Vaiṣṇava
author_bio: Narahari is known for writing the Bodhasāra, a text on philosophical and spiritual wisdom. He is associated with Hindu scholastic traditions of the late medieval period, though precise biographical information remains limited.
author_notes: Attribution is secure from surviving manuscripts, but the author's life is poorly documented; the name may refer to multiple individuals across genres.

author_bio: Nārāyaṇa is credited with the composition of the Hitopadeśa, a popular collection of fables and moral stories in Sanskrit. The text's authorship is traditionally assigned to Nārāyaṇa, but virtually no independent biographical information survives, and the name may be a literary attribution.
author_notes: The author_display_name is a traditional attribution for the Hitopadeśa; the historical person is elusive and the name may be conventional or pseudonymous.

author_bio: Nārāyaṇabhaṭṭa was a Sanskrit poet and scholar from Kerala, best known for the Nārāyaṇīya, a devotional work celebrating the life and deeds of Kṛṣṇa.
author_notes: Well-attested figure in Kerala literary history; the Nārāyaṇīya is widely recited.

author_bio: Indian Buddhist mahasiddha and scholar, a key figure in the transmission of tantric teachings, particularly the Kālacakra tradition, and a teacher of Marpa.
author_notes: Attestation in Tibetan and Sanskrit sources; historical figure with hagiographic elements.

author_bio: Nīlakaṇṭhadīkṣita was a Sanskrit poet and scholar in the court of the Madurai Nāyaks. His satirical work Kaliviḍambana critiques contemporary society, and he also composed panegyric and philosophical verse.
author_notes: Known from internal evidence and court records; a well-documented figure in early modern South India.

author_bio: Padmagupta was a Sanskrit poet at the Paramāra court, celebrated for his epic poem Navasāhasāṅkacarita, which narrates the exploits of King Muñja. His work exemplifies the ornate kāvya style.
author_notes: Sometimes confused with later poets; identity attested primarily through the Navasāhasāṅkacarita and references in literary tradition.

author_cultural_context: Buddhist? Nyāya? Possible overlapping schools; commentary on the Avayavinirākaraṇa suggests logical and epistemological interest
author_bio: Paṇḍitāśoka is known for the Avayavinirākaraṇa (a refutation of the doctrine of the whole) and the Sāmānyadūṣaṇadikprasāritā, texts in Indian philosophical debate. His works engage with Nyāya and Buddhist atomism.
author_notes: Very little biographical data; identification as 'Paṇḍitāśoka' suggests a link to the Buddhist lineage but remains uncertain.

author_bio: Pāṇini is the author of the Aṣṭādhyāyī, a comprehensive and highly systematic grammar of Sanskrit that remains foundational to linguistic science and the study of ancient Indian knowledge systems.
author_notes: Virtually nothing is known of his life outside his work; his grammar has been the subject of extensive commentary for over two millennia.

author_bio: Pārthasārathimiśra was a prominent Mīmāṃsā philosopher and commentator, best known for his Śāstradīpikā, a lucid exposition of Pūrva Mīmāṃsā. He belonged to the early medieval period of Indian philosophy and wrote in Sanskrit.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure with clear known works and traditional biographical details.

author_bio: Patañjali is the traditional compiler of the Yogasūtras, a foundational text of the Yoga darśana. He is also known as a grammarian for the Mahābhāṣya.
author_notes: Multiple figures named Patañjali; identity as single author is debated but widely accepted.

author_bio: The Pauṣkarasaṃhitā is a Sanskrit text of the Pāñcarātra tradition, a religious corpus regarded as revealed scripture. It prescribes temple worship and theological doctrine for Vaiṣṇava communities.
author_notes: As a work title occupying the author slot, it is a text regarded as divinely revealed; no human author is claimed.

author_bio: Prabodhānandasarasvatī was a 16th-century Sanskrit poet and theologian in the Gaudiya Vaishnava tradition, best known for the Caitanyacandrāmṛta, a devotional poem celebrating the life of Caitanya Mahāprabhu.
author_notes: Historically attested author in the Caitanya tradition; clear attribution.

author_bio: The Prātimokṣasūtra is a foundational Buddhist disciplinary code listing monastic rules, recited in the uposatha ceremony across multiple Buddhist traditions.
author_notes: The text is treated as an author entry because it functions as the attributed source; no single human author is known.

author_bio: Author referred to as Pseudo-Jonarāja; likely a later writer continuing the Rājataraṅginī tradition under the name of the historical Jonarāja.
author_notes: The 'Pseudo-' prefix indicates contested or false attribution; scholarly consensus distinguishes this author from the historical Jonarāja.

author_cultural_context: Vedānta tradition, compiler of the Brahmasūtra
author_bio: Bādarāyaṇa is the traditional author of the Brahmasūtra (also known as Vedāntasūtra), a foundational text of the Vedānta school. Though details of his life are obscure, he is venerated as a key figure in the development of systematic Vedānta philosophy.
author_notes: Attribution is solidly traditional; the Brahmasūtra is universally assigned to this figure. The name may also be synonymous with Vyāsa in some traditions.

author_cultural_context: Sanskrit court poet, Harsha's court, Kannauj
author_bio: Bāṇa (also known as Bāṇabhaṭṭa) was a 7th-century Sanskrit prose writer and court poet of Emperor Harshavardhana. He is celebrated for the Harshacharita, a biography of his patron, and the Kadambari, a romance novel. His works are foundational in Sanskrit prose kāvya.
author_notes: The representative title 'Harṣacaritapariśiṣṭa' appears to be a supplementary or related text; Bāṇa's primary corpus is well-established.

author_cultural_context: Poet of the court of King Harṣa, Sanskrit literature
author_bio: Bāṇabhaṭṭa was a renowned Sanskrit poet and prose writer, best known for his Harṣacarita, a biography of King Harṣa, and the Kādambarī. He served as a court poet and is celebrated for his ornate style and vivid descriptions.
author_notes: Historically attested figure with secure attribution and multiple surviving works.

author_bio: Sanskrit poet from Kashmir, author of the celebrated Caurapañcaśikā (Fifty Verses of the Thief), a lyrical poem of illicit love; also wrote the historical Vikramāṅkadevacarita.
author_notes: Attested in historical records as a court poet under the Calukya king Vikramāditya VI.

author_bio: The *Brahmapurāṇa* is one of the eighteen major Purāṇas, a compendium of mythology, cosmology, genealogy, and religious instruction attributed to the god Brahmā. It is a composite text, gradually expanded over centuries.
author_notes: Author slot is the text itself (a purāṇa); no human author is known. The text is a collective, incremental composition.

author_cultural_context: Buddhist; associated with Mahāyāna traditions and possibly Nālandā monastery
author_bio: Buddhaghoṣa is a Buddhist scholar-monk, author of the *Siddhārthacaritrakāvya*, a poetic biography of the Buddha. Not to be confused with the famous Pāli commentator Buddhaghosa; this Sanskrit author belongs to a different lineage, active in the Gupta period.
author_notes: Historical but ambiguous; the name 'Buddhaghoṣa' occurs in multiple Buddhist traditions. This row is distinguished by the Sanskrit work *Siddhārthacaritrakāvya*.

author_bio: Budhasvāmin is credited as the author of the Bṛhatkathāślokasaṃgraha, an epitome of the now-lost Bṛhatkathā. His identity is largely inferred from textual tradition and may be a composite figure.
author_notes: Scholars debate whether Budhasvāmin is a historical person or a traditional attribution; the name may derive from narrative conventions.

author_bio: Bhallaṭa was a Kashmiri Sanskrit poet, author of the *Bhallaṭaśataka*, a century of verses known for their elegant style and emotional depth. He is often cited in anthologies and works on poetics.
author_notes: Historical figure, but biographical details are sparse; his fame rests almost entirely on the *Bhallaṭaśataka*.

author_bio: Bharata Muni is the legendary sage credited with composing the Nāṭyaśāstra, the foundational treatise on Sanskrit theatre and performance. His historicity is uncertain.
author_notes: Traditional authorship; likely a composite figure or project name.

author_bio: Bhāravi is a celebrated Sanskrit poet of the classical period, known solely for his epic poem Kirātārjunīya, which dramatizes Arjuna's encounter with Śiva.
author_notes: Securely historical; work widely cited in alamkāraśāstra.

author_region: Indian subcontinent (possibly Kashmir or central India)
author_cultural_context: Sanskrit grammarian and philosopher; author of the *Vākyapadīya* and the *Śatakatraya*
author_bio: Bhartṛhari is a foundational figure in Sanskrit linguistics and philosophy, known for his *Vākyapadīya* on the philosophy of language. He is also credited with the *Śatakatraya*, a collection of three centuries of moral and erotic verse that reflect a worldly and ascetic outlook.
author_notes: The identity of the grammarian and the poet has been debated, but current scholarship tends to accept them as the same historical figure. The *Śatakatraya* is widely transmitted and anthologized.

author_bio: Bhāsa is one of the earliest known Sanskrit playwrights, traditionally dated to the early centuries CE. He authored at least thirteen plays, of which the Dūtavākya is a representative work. Though few biographical details survive, his dramas are valued for their dramatic structure and engagement with epic themes.
author_notes: Attested as a foundational figure in classical Sanskrit drama; attribution of plays has been debated but largely accepted.

author_bio: Bhāskarakaṇṭha was a Sanskrit commentator and philosopher active in the late medieval period. He is best known for his commentary (Mokṣopāyaṭīkā) on the Mokṣopāya (Yogavāsiṣṭha), an important work of Advaita Vedantic and Śaiva thought.
author_notes: Biographical details are limited; his prominence rests on the preservation and transmission of his commentary.

author_bio: Bhaṭṭi is a Sanskrit poet best known for the Rāvaṇavadha (also called Bhaṭṭikāvya), a poetic retelling of the Rāmāyaṇa that simultaneously illustrates grammatical rules. It is a unique blend of literature and pedagogy.
author_notes: The attribution to Bhaṭṭi is traditional; little is known about his life.

author_bio: Bhāvāgaṇeśa was an early modern Indian scholar who wrote the Tattvayāthārthyadīpana, a commentary on Advaita Vedanta. His works engage with post-Śaṅkara debates on reality and illusion.
author_notes: Relatively obscure figure; known mainly through his surviving commentaries.

author_bio: Bhāvamiśra was a physician and scholar, author of the Bhāvaprakāśa, a major Ayurvedic compendium. He synthesized earlier medical knowledge and is cited in later traditions.
author_notes: Well-known figure in Ayurvedic literature. The Bhāvaprakāśa is a standard reference.

author_bio: Bhavatrāta was a commentator on the Jaiminīyaśrautasūtra, active in the Vedic ritual tradition. His work survives in multiple manuscripts.
author_notes: Limited biographical data; name known only through colophons.

author_bio: Paramāra king of Malwa and prolific polymath, patron of arts and letters, author of the philosophical commentary Rājamārtaṇḍa on the Yoga Sūtras.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure with multiple extant works; attribution of works varies.

author_bio: Mādhava was a 14th-century Indian scholar and minister in the Vijayanagara Empire, often identified with the commentator Mādhavācārya. He wrote the Jaiminīyanyāyamālāvistara and related works on Mimamsa epistemology, and is also linked with the Sarvadarśanasaṃgraha.
author_notes: Important historical figure; sometimes conflated with Mādhavācārya; his authorship of the compendium is debated.

author_bio: Mādhavācārya is widely regarded as the author of the Sarvadarśanasaṃgraha, a compendium of Indian philosophical systems, and is often identified with the scholar Mādhava of Vijayanagara. His work provides a landmark doxographical survey.
author_notes: Historical figure; the identity Mādhavācārya sometimes overlaps with Mādhava; the compendium is a key source for Indian philosophy.

author_bio: Madhusūdanasarasvatī was a leading philosopher of Advaita Vedanta and a devotee of Kṛṣṇa. His Siddhāntabindu is a commentary on the Daśaślokī of Śaṅkara, and his works bridge non-dualism and bhakti.
author_notes: Extremely well-documented figure. His works are central to later Advaita and bhakti traditions.

author_bio: Mādhva (also known as Madhvācārya) was a Vaiṣṇava philosopher and theologian who founded the Dvaita school of Vedānta. His works include commentaries on the Mahābhārata (Mahābhāratatātparyanirṇaya) and systematic treatises such as the Anuvyākhyāna. He is a central figure in the history of Indian philosophy.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; traditional dates are accepted in scholarly consensus.

author_bio: Mādhva Ānandatīrtha is the founder of the Dvaita school of Vedānta. He authored numerous works, including the Kṛṣṇāmṛtamahārṇava, a devotional poem on Kṛṣṇa.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure with a substantial biographical and textual tradition.

author_bio: Māgha is a renowned Sanskrit poet, author of the epic *Śiśupālavadha* (The Slaying of Śiśupāla), a mahākāvya celebrated for its verbal artistry and complex syntax. He is considered one of the great classical poets alongside Kālidāsa and Bhāravi.
author_notes: Little is known of his life, but his work is securely attributed and widely cited in the Sanskrit poetic tradition. The *Śiśupālavadha* is a model of the mahākāvya genre.

author_bio: The Mahāsubhāṣitasaṃgraha is a large Sanskrit anthology of gnomic verses (subhāṣitas) from a wide range of authors. The compilation itself is anonymous and traditionally cited by its title.
author_notes: The author slot is occupied by the work title as the compilation has no known single compiler.

author_bio: Maitreya is a legendary figure in Buddhist tradition, regarded as the source of the Yogācāra teachings embodied in the Madhyāntavibhāgakārikā, transmitted through Asaṅga.
author_notes: Attribution is traditional; Maitreya is considered a celestial bodhisattva or a human teacher in legend.

author_bio: Maitreyavyākaraṇa is the title of a Mahāyāna sūtra concerning the future Buddha Maitreya. It is a anonymous scriptural text, transmitted in Sanskrit.
author_notes: The author slot in catalog records is occupied by the title itself as the text is traditionally not attributed to a named human author.

author_cultural_context: Hindu; Mīmāṃsā and Vedānta; known for his synthesis of Bhāṭṭa Mīmāṃsā and Advaita
author_bio: Maṇḍanamiśra was a major philosopher and author of the Brahmasiddhi, a seminal text of the Advaita Vedānta tradition. He also wrote the Vibhramaviveka, an analysis of error (khyāti). His work mediates between Mīmāṃsā and Advaita epistemology.
author_notes: Sometimes identified with Sureśvara, though traditional accounts vary. Strong evidence for floruit in the 8th century.

author_bio: The Mārkaṇḍeyapurāṇa is one of the major Purāṇas, attributed to the sage Mārkaṇḍeya in tradition. It contains hymns, cosmological stories, and the influential Devī Māhātmya section glorifying the goddess.
author_notes: The suffix '1' may indicate a text variant or manuscript grouping. 'Mārkaṇḍeyapurāṇa' is a title, not a personal author.

author_bio: Maskari is a commentator on the Gautamasmṛti, a legal text. His bhāṣya is an important source for the interpretation of early dharma literature.
author_notes: Known only through his commentary; biographical details are scarce.

author_bio: Māṭhara is known for the Māṭharavṛtti, a commentary on the Sāṃkhyakārikā. He is considered an important exponent of the Sāṃkhya school, though details of his life are sparse, with attribution relying on his surviving textual work.
author_notes: Attribution is based on textual tradition; independent biographical evidence is lacking, leading to some uncertainty about precise dating.

author_bio: Yājñavalkya is a legendary Vedic sage and philosopher, central to the Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad, and traditionally credited with the Yājñavalkyasmṛti, a key dharmaśāstra text.
author_notes: The sage figure is distinct from the later legal author; the Yājñavalkyasmṛti is a medieval composition attributed to him.

author_cultural_context: Vedānta and Āḻvār tradition, teacher of Śrīvaiṣṇavism
author_bio: Yāmuna (also known as Yāmunācārya) was a significant early theologian of the Śrīvaiṣṇava tradition, authoring works such as Ātmasiddhi, Saṃvitsiddhi, and Catuḥślokī. He is known for bridging the devotional Āḻvār poetry with systematic Vedānta philosophy.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure with multiple surviving works, referenced extensively in later Śrīvaiṣṇava commentaries.

author_bio: Yāska is an ancient Sanskrit grammarian and etymologist, author of the Nirukta, the foundational text of Vedic etymology and one of the six Vedangas.
author_notes: Well-attested as a historical figure; the Nirukta is his sole surviving work.

author_bio: Legendary female mahasiddha of the Buddhist tantric tradition, credited with the Advayasiddhi; her life story is rich in hagiographic elements.
author_notes: Historicity uncertain; known almost entirely from Tibetan hagiographies and a single extant Sanskrit work.

author_bio: Rādhākṛṣṇadāsagosvāmin was a Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇava author and devotee, known for the Sādhanadīpikā, a manual of devotional practice. His works reflect the Bhakti traditions centered on Rādhā and Kṛṣṇa.
author_notes: Often linked to the Gosvāmī lineage; biographical details are sparse and reliant on tradition.

author_bio: Rāghavacaitanya is known for works on the goddess Mahāgaṇapati, including a stotra and commentary. Little historical evidence exists for his life; he may have been a tantric practitioner in the Ganapatya tradition.
author_notes: The attribution is based on manuscript colophons. The name suggests a devotional lineage, but biographical details are lacking.

author_bio: Raghunāthadāsagosvāmin was a 16th-century Gaudiya Vaishnava saint and writer, one of the six Gosvāmins of Vrindavan, and author of the Muktācarita and other devotional works.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure within the Caitanya tradition; hagiography does not obscure his historicity.

author_bio: Rāmānuja was a towering theologian and philosopher of the Sri Vaishnava tradition, foundational to the Vishishtadvaita school of Vedanta. His commentaries, notably on the Bhagavadgītā and the Vedānta-sūtras, shaped devotional Vedanta for centuries.
author_notes: Historically well-attested; hagiographies exist but do not obscure the historical figure.

author_bio: Ratnākaraśānti was a Buddhist scholar active in the Pāla period. He wrote on Buddhist philosophy and tantra, and his work Sāratama is a commentary related to the Guhyasamāja tradition.
author_notes: Attested in Tibetan and Sanskrit sources; details of his life remain fragmentary.

author_bio: Ratnakīrti was a Buddhist logician and philosopher associated with the Vikramaśīla monastery in eastern India. A prominent figure in the pramāṇa tradition, he authored the *Apohasiddhi*, a key text on the theory of exclusion (apoha) as a semantic strategy, defending Dignāga and Dharmakīrti's legacy.
author_notes: Ratnakīrti is securely attested in Buddhist doxographical and commentarial literature. His works are studied within specialist circles of Buddhist philosophy.

author_bio: Ravideva was a Jain commentator and poet. He is known for his commentary on the Rākṣasakāvya and for composing the Rākṣasakāvya itself, a Jain retelling of narrative themes.
author_notes: Limited biographical information; the attribution of both the commentary and the base text to the same author requires further verification.

author_bio: Rudrabhaṭṭa was a Sanskrit poet and scholar, known for his work Śṛṅgāratilaka, a treatise on erotic poetry and aesthetic theory. He belongs to the early medieval tradition of Indian kāvya and alaṅkāra literature.
author_notes: Well-attested figure within Sanskrit literary circles; his works are cited by later commentators.

author_bio: Rūpagosvāmin was a leading theologian and poet of the Gaudiya Vaishnava tradition, a close associate of Caitanya. His Haribhaktirasāmṛtasindhu is a foundational work on bhakti-rasa, and the Laghubhāgavatāmṛta summarizes Vaishnava theology.
author_notes: Extremely well-documented historical figure. The two major works are central to Gaudiya tradition.

author_bio: Ruyyaka was a Kashmiri Sanskrit poet and literary theorist, best known for his work on poetics and alaṅkāraśāstra, particularly the *Sahṛdayalīlā*, a commentary on Mammaṭa's *Kāvyaprakāśa*. His contributions reflect the vibrant Kashmiri tradition of aesthetic theory.
author_notes: Well-attested in Sanskrit literary and commentarial traditions, though not widely known beyond scholarly circles.

author_bio: A name associated with the Śaiva deity or a legendary sage, traditionally credited with the Śivastuti; possibly a divine epithet rather than a historical author.
author_notes: The name may refer to a figure from the Rāmāyaṇa; historicity unclear, likely legendary or mythic.

author_bio: The heading 'Larger' refers to the Longer (or Larger) Prajñāpāramitā Sūtra, a Mahayana scripture, and is not a personal author but a textual work occupying the author slot.
author_notes: This entry is a title-based author heading for the Larger Prajñāpāramitā, not an individual person. 'Larger' is a conventional descriptor for the expanded version of the sūtra, likely from a catalog of anonymous or traditio-nal scriptures.

author_bio: Lāṭyāyana is the author of an important Śrautasūtra belonging to the Sāmavedic tradition, guiding Vedic ritual performance.
author_notes: Name refers to a preceptor figure; work extant and cited in ritual compendia.

author_bio: Lolimbarāja was a Sanskrit medical writer, best known for his concise āyurvedic compendium Vaidyajīvana ('Life of a Physician'), which gained wide popularity for its practical therapeutic guidance.
author_notes: Authorship is generally accepted, though some biographical details remain uncertain.

author_cultural_context: Nyaya and Vedanta philosophy; syncretic scholarship
author_bio: Vācaspatimiśra was a prolific Indian philosopher who wrote commentaries across multiple schools, including the Bhāmati on Śaṅkara's Vedānta and the Nyāyavārttikatātparyaṭīkā on Nyāya, known for his encyclopedic breadth.
author_notes: Historically well-attested; no significant doubt about identity or period.

author_bio: Vādivāgīśvara was a Jaina logician and author of the Mānamanohara, a manual of Jaina logic (Nyāya). He belonged to the scholastic tradition that systematized Jaina epistemology.
author_notes: Little is known outside his works; attribution is secure but biographical details are scarce.

author_cultural_context: Āyurvedic tradition; Buddhist or Brahminical background debated
author_bio: Vāgbhaṭa is a major figure in classical Āyurveda, author of the *Aṣṭāṅgahṛdayasaṃhitā* and *Aṣṭāṅgasaṃgraha*. These compendia systematize the medical teachings of Caraka and Suśruta, and Vāgbhaṭa's works remain foundational in Indian medicine.
author_notes: Historical person; identity is well-established although some details of biography (e.g., exact region) remain debated.

author_bio: Vāgīśvarakīrti was a Buddhist scholar associated with tantric ritual texts, notably the Mṛtyuvañcanopadeśa, a manual on averting death.
author_notes: Name includes the honorific -kīrti typical of Buddhist authors.

author_bio: Vallabhadeva was a 10th-century Kashmiri poet and anthologist, best known for compiling the Subhāṣitāvali, a celebrated anthology of Sanskrit subhāṣita (wise sayings) verses, which provides a valuable window into the aesthetic and ethical values of the period.
author_notes: Well-attested figure in the history of Sanskrit anthologies; his compilation is a standard reference.

author_cultural_context: Hindu epic tradition, sage-poet, author of the Rāmāyaṇa
author_bio: Vālmīki is traditionally hailed as the ādi kavi (first poet) of Sanskrit literature and the author of the Rāmāyaṇa. His biography is largely legendary, with accounts describing him as a reformed bandit turned sage. Despite the mythic elements, the Rāmāyaṇa has profoundly influenced Indian culture.
author_notes: Historicity uncertain; figure is surrounded by legend. Attribution of the entire Rāmāyaṇa may mask multiple authors and centuries of redaction.

author_bio: Vāmanadatta was a Kashmiri Śaiva philosopher and poet, known for works such as the Svabodhodayamañjarī and Dvayasaṃpattivārttika, reflecting Advaita and Śaiva thought.
author_notes: Attribution is based on manuscript colophons; some works may be composite.

author_bio: The Vāmanapurāṇa is a Purāṇic text centered on the dwarf avatar Vāmana of Viṣṇu. It is a composite scripture with multiple layers of redaction, not attributable to a single author.
author_notes: This entry represents the text itself in the author slot. The suffix '1' indicates disambiguation; the Purāṇa is well-known as a named work.

author_cultural_context: Hindu; grammatical tradition (Pāṇinian school); associated with the Sāhityaśāstrī region
author_bio: Varadarāja was a Sanskrit grammarian best known for the Laghusiddhāntakaumudī, a concise version of Bhaṭṭoji Dīkṣita's Siddhāntakaumudī. His works serve as pedagogical tools for Pāṇinian grammar.
author_notes: Firm dates lacking; often placed in the 17th century based on the grammatical lineage.

author_cultural_context: Hindu; Śrauta ritual tradition; possibly from South India
author_bio: Varadattasuta is a commentator on the Śāṅkhāyanaśrautasūtra, the Vedic manual for the solemn rituals of the Śāṅkhāyana tradition. His bhāṣya is a detailed scholastic exposition.
author_notes: Patronymic name ('son of Varadatta'); biographical details scanty. Regional and period are conjectural.

author_bio: Varāhamihira was a celebrated Indian astronomer and astrologer at the court of the Gupta king Vikramāditya. His magnum opus Bṛhatsaṃhitā covers a wide range of topics including astronomy, astrology, architecture, and natural sciences, and his Yogayātrā is a key astrological text.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure with multiple surviving works.

author_bio: Vararuci is a name attached to several works in Sanskrit, including the Śatagāthā. The historical identity is uncertain, with traditions conflating him with grammarians and poets. His attributed works show a blend of literary and didactic themes.
author_notes: The name Vararuci appears in multiple contexts; the attribution to a single historical figure is unclear. The Śatagāthā is a collection of 100 verses, often linked to the Kathā tradition.

author_cultural_context: Indian subcontinent; Indian Buddhist philosophy, Yogācāra school
author_bio: Vasubandhu was one of the most influential thinkers in Buddhist philosophy, authoring foundational Yogācāra works and the Abhidharmakośa. He is traditionally said to have converted from Sarvāstivāda to Mahāyāna.
author_notes: While some works attributed to Vasubandhu may be by different authors, the core works and biographical tradition are well-established.

author_cultural_context: Indian subcontinent; classical Hindu philosophy; Brahmanical and Buddhist traditions
author_bio: Vātsyāyana is best known as the author of the Kāmasūtra, a seminal text on erotic love and social conduct, and for his commentary on the Nyāyasūtras, the foundational text of the Nyāya school of logic. He flourished around the Gupta period in classical India.
author_notes: Both works traditionally attributed to a single author; some scholars question identity across genres.

author_bio: Vedānta Deśika (also known as Veṅkaṭanātha) was a prolific Śrīvaiṣṇava theologian, poet, and philosopher. He wrote in Sanskrit, Tamil, and Prakrit, producing works on philosophy, poetry, and devotional literature. His Haṃsasaṃdeśa is a classic sandeśa kāvya.
author_notes: One work recorded; the Haṃsasaṃdeśa is a minor but representative poem in his vast corpus.

author_bio: Vidyākaraśānti was a Buddhist scholar who wrote the Tarkasopāna, a manual on logic and reasoning within the Buddhist philosophical tradition.
author_notes: Name indicates a monastic or teacher; work shows influence of the Buddhist epistemological school.

author_bio: Vijñānabhikṣu was a philosopher who wrote the Sāṃkhyasāra and other works, synthesizing Sāṃkhya, Yoga, and Vedānta in a non-dual framework.
author_notes: Well-known as a commentator and independent thinker; works widely cited in later traditions.

author_bio: Vijñāneśvara was an 11th-century Indian jurist and scholar, best known for composing the Mitākṣarā, a seminal commentary on the Yājñavalkya Smṛti that became a foundational text of Hindu law.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure; major commentator in the Dharmashastra tradition.

author_bio: Vimalamitra was an Indian Buddhist scholar who traveled to Tibet and contributed to the transmission of Buddhist teachings, especially Dzogchen. The Abhidharmadīpa attributed to him is a later commentary on the Abhidharma.
author_notes: The Abhidharmadīpa is often attributed to a different Vimalamitra; the identity may be conflated with multiple figures of the same name.

author_cultural_context: Indian subcontinent; Sanskrit poetics and literary theory
author_bio: Viśvanāthakavirāja was a Sanskrit poet and rhetorician, best known for his Sāhityadarpaṇa, a comprehensive treatise on Sanskrit literary theory that remains a standard introduction to alamkāraśāstra.
author_notes: Sāhityadarpaṇa is securely attributed to him.

author_bio: Viśveśvarakavicandra is a Sanskrit poet and scholar, best known for the Camatkāracandrikā, a work on literary aesthetics and drama.
author_notes: Known primarily from his sole extant work; biographical data are limited.

author_bio: Vyāsa is the legendary compiler of the Mahābhārata and the traditional author of the Bhagavadgītā and the Purāṇas, regarded as a semidivine sage (rṣi) in the Hindu tradition.
author_notes: Vyāsa is a traditional figure with legendary biography; attribution is traditio-nal rather than historical. The identity likely represents a lineage of bards and redactors.

author_bio: Śaṃkara (Śaṃkarācārya) is the most influential philosopher of Advaita Vedānta, renowned for his commentaries on the Upaniṣads, Brahmasūtra, and Bhagavadgītā, and for founding monastic centers across India.
author_notes: Large corpus; traditional dates may be imprecise but historicity is secure.
representative_titles: Chāndogyopaniṣadbhāṣya | Śārīrakamīmāṃsābhāṣya | Īśopaniṣadbhāṣya | Kaṭhopaniṣadbhāṣya | Bṛhadāraṇyakopaniṣadbhāṣya | Taittirīyopaniṣadbhāṣya | Praśnopaniṣadbhāṣya | Aitareyopaniṣadbhāṣya

author_bio: Śāntideva was an 8th-century Indian Buddhist monk and scholar at Nalanda, renowned for his Bodhicaryāvatāra, a classic text on the bodhisattva path and the cultivation of compassion and wisdom.
author_notes: Historical figure; widely venerated within Tibetan and East Asian Buddhism.

author_bio: The Śatasāhasrikāprajñāpāramitā ('Perfection of Wisdom in One Hundred Thousand Lines') is a massive Mahāyāna Buddhist sūtra, part of the larger Prajñāpāramitā corpus. It expounds the concept of emptiness (śūnyatā) and the bodhisattva path, and is traditionally considered a revealed text rather than the work of a named human author.
author_notes: The author_display_name is the title of a canonical text; no human author is attributed. Categorized as work_title per instructions.

author_bio: This entry represents the Śivapurāṇa, a major Puranic text dedicated to the god Śiva, used as a collective author heading for its constituent versions and manuscripts.
author_notes: The heading 'Śivapurāṇabooks' is a catalog label for the textual corpus of the Śivapurāṇa, not an individual author. It is treated as a work_title author agent kind.

author_bio: Śrīkaṇṭha is a Śaiva author of the Ratnatrayaparīkṣā, a work examining the three jewels (pati, paśu, pāśa) from a Śaiva Siddhānta perspective.
author_notes: Name may be confused with other Śrīkaṇṭhas; specific context points to Śaiva Siddhānta.

author_bio: Ṣimānanda is a later Sāṃkhya philosopher, author of the Sāṃkhyatattvavivecana, a commentary on classical Sāṃkhya teachings.
author_notes: Very little biographical data survives; the dating is approximate.

author_bio: Samantabhadra was a Digambara Jain philosopher and poet, author of the Āptamīmāṃsā, a foundational text on Jain epistemology and the doctrine of the omniscient being. He is revered as an ācārya in the Jain tradition.
author_notes: Dates vary widely; sometimes placed in the 2nd–5th centuries. Not to be confused with the Buddha Samantabhadra.

author_bio: Somadeva was an 11th-century Kashmiri Brahmin and court poet, best known for his vast story collection the *Kathāsaritsāgara* ('Ocean of Streams of Stories'), a retelling of the lost *Bṛhatkathā* of Guṇāḍhya. His work preserves a rich tapestry of Indian folk tales, frame narratives, and Śaiva devotional elements.
author_notes: Historical person; author's name is well-attested in manuscript traditions and literary histories. The single work is his magnum opus.

author_bio: Sthiramati was a 6th-century Indian Buddhist philosopher and commentator in the Yogacara tradition, known for his commentaries on Vasubandhu's works, including the Triṃśikāvijñaptibhāṣya.
author_notes: Well-attested historical figure in Buddhist doxographical sources and Chinese pilgrimage accounts.

author_bio: Subandhu was an early Indian poet and prose writer, author of the Vāsavadattā, a romantic narrative that marks an early phase of Sanskrit literary prose (gadya-kāvya).
author_notes: Attested historical figure; known for a single major work, a landmark in Sanskrit fiction.

author_cultural_context: Indian subcontinent; Mīmāṃsā school of exegesis
author_bio: Sucaritamiśra was a Mīmāṃsā philosopher from Kashmir, best known for his Kāśikā commentary on Śabara's commentary on the Mīmāṃsā Sūtras. His work is central to the Prābhākara tradition.
author_notes: Well-attested figure in Mīmāṃsā commentarial lineage.

author_bio: Sulhaṇa was a medieval Sanskrit commentator and poet, known for the Sukavihṛdayānandinī, a commentary on the Kāvyaprakāśa, and other works on poetics.
author_notes: Not widely known; limited biographical evidence; works survive in specialized traditions.

author_bio: Suśruta is a legendary physician and surgeon, traditionally regarded as the author of the Suśrutasaṃhitā, a foundational Ayurvedic text on surgery and medicine. The historical person behind the name is uncertain, but the text dates to the early centuries CE.
author_notes: The author's historicity is uncertain; the text is a composite work attributed to a legendary sage.

author_bio: Svātmārāma is a little-known medieval yogi attributed with the Haṭhayogapradīpikā, a seminal text on haṭha yoga. The work compiles earlier teachings and became a foundational manual for physical yogic practices.
author_notes: Historical details are sparse; the attribution rests on the text's colophon and internal evidence.

author_cultural_context: Indian subcontinent; Jaina scholar and philosopher
author_bio: Haribhadrasūri was a prolific Jaina writer and philosopher, composing works on logic, philosophy, and doxography. He is known for his synthetic approach and for the Yogabindu and numerous commentaries.
author_notes: Well-attested Jaina author; some minor works may be attributed to a later namesake.

author_cultural_context: Itihāsa-Purāṇa tradition, appendix to the Mahābhārata
author_bio: The *Harivaṃśa* is a Sanskrit text traditionally considered an appendix (khila) to the Mahābhārata. It narrates the genealogy of Viṣṇu, the life of Kṛṣṇa, and the cosmic cycles, blending epic, purāṇic, and devotional elements. It is a key source for early Kṛṣṇa mythology.
author_notes: The work is attributed to Vyāsa in tradition but is clearly the product of multiple authors over time. Catalog entries often treat 'Harivaṃśa' as the author entity for the text itself.

author_bio: Harivarman was a Buddhist philosopher associated with the Mādhyamaka tradition, author of the Satyasiddhiśāstra, a major compendium of Buddhist doctrine.
author_notes: Known primarily through Chinese and Tibetan translations; original Sanskrit scarce.

author_bio: Harṣadeva is a Sanskrit dramatist, traditionally credited with the play Nāgānanda, a poetic work blending Buddhist and Śaiva themes.
author_notes: Sometimes conflated with the emperor Harṣavardhana; attribution of Nāgānanda is debated.

author_bio: Hemacandra was a Jain polymath, grammarian, and poet, active in the Chaulukya court. He authored the *Abhidhānacintāmaṇi*, a Sanskrit lexicon, along with works on grammar, prosody, and Jain philosophy. He is one of the most important figures in Jain Sanskrit literature.
author_notes: Well-documented historical figure with a vast corpus; here represented by a single lexical work.
